verses.text: When all the kings of the Amorites west of Jordan, all the kings of the Canaanites by the sea, heard that the LORD had dried up the waters of Jordan from before the children of Israel, until we were passed over, their heart melted, neither was there spirit in them any more, because of the children of Israel. Guidance: News travels faster than armies. The Canaanite kings hear of the dry crossing and lose their nerve before a single arrow is shot. God had said the terror would precede them (Exod 23:27, Deut 11:25). The first weapon in the conquest is not steel — it is reputation, and the reputation is the LORD's.

At that time the LORD said unto Joshua, Make thee sharp knives, and circumcise again the children of Israel the second time. Joshua made him sharp knives, and circumcised the children of Israel at the hill of the foreskins. This is the cause: all the people that came out of Egypt, the men of war, died in the wilderness; all those were circumcised, but all the people born in the wilderness were not. The children of Israel walked forty years in the wilderness, till all the men of war that came out of Egypt were consumed, because they obeyed not the voice of the LORD: their children, whom he raised up in their stead, Joshua circumcised: for they were uncircumcised, because they had not been circumcised by the way. Guidance: First act in Canaan is not battle — it is surgery. A whole generation made vulnerable on the wrong side of the river, within sight of Jericho's walls. Faith makes the army weak before it can be strong. The covenant sign is restored before the covenant land is taken. You don't fight for an inheritance you haven't yet identified as belonging to your God.

When they had done circumcising all the people, they abode in their places in the camp, till they were whole. Guidance: A camped army with bandages on, miles from Jericho. The Canaanite king could have raided that week and ended Israel before Jericho fell. He did not. The terror of v1 holds; God guards the wound while it heals. Sometimes God shelters you precisely while you are most exposed because you obeyed Him.

The LORD said unto Joshua, This day have I rolled away the reproach of Egypt from off you. Wherefore the name of the place is called Gilgal unto this day. Guidance: Gilgal — from the verb 'to roll.' The shame of Egypt rolled off like a stone. For forty years the Egyptians could say, He brought them out to kill them in the desert (Exod 32:12, Deut 9:28). The river crossing answered that taunt; circumcision sealed the answer. God names the place after a verb of removal — every time Israel says 'Gilgal,' they say 'rolled away.'

The children of Israel encamped in Gilgal, and kept the passover on the fourteenth day of the month at even in the plains of Jericho. Guidance: The first Passover in the land — and the first since the wilderness. The 14th of the first month: forty years to the day from the lamb in Egypt. They left Egypt with a lamb; they enter Canaan with a lamb. The bookends of the journey are blood on a doorpost and a feast on a battlefield.

They did eat of the old corn of the land on the morrow after the passover, unleavened cakes and parched corn in the selfsame day. The manna ceased on the morrow after they had eaten of the old corn of the land; neither had the children of Israel manna any more; but they did eat of the fruit of the land of Canaan that year. Guidance: Manna stops the day the harvest starts. Forty years of bread from heaven, ended without ceremony, because it is no longer needed. God's gifts often have a season. The miracle was never to keep them dependent on miracle; it was to deliver them to an ordinary table where they could eat what their hands had not yet planted.

When Joshua was by Jericho, he lifted up his eyes and looked: there stood a man over against him with his sword drawn in his hand. Joshua went unto him: Art thou for us, or for our adversaries? He said: Nay; but as captain of the host of the LORD am I now come. Joshua fell on his face to the earth, did worship: What saith my lord unto his servant? The captain said: Loose thy shoe from off thy foot; for the place whereon thou standest is holy. Joshua did so. Guidance: Joshua expects two camps; the visitor will not be assigned to either. 'Nay; but I am come' — the LORD does not join sides, He commands the army. The new Moses repeats the old burning-bush instruction: take off the shoe. The conquest does not begin with Joshua's strategy session — it begins with him face-down and barefoot, learning that this war is the LORD's.
